text: A 42-year-old patient presents with chronic fatigue, joint pain, and a butterfly-shaped facial rash. These symptoms could each have independent causes, or they could share a common underlying condition. Analyse whether and how these symptoms connect, identifying the most likely unifying diagnosis and explaining the causal pathway.
ground_truth: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE/Lupus) connects all three: autoimmune inflammation causes the butterfly rash (malar rash), joint pain (lupus arthritis), and fatigue (chronic inflammation + possible anaemia). Treating them independently would miss the underlying autoimmune process.
scoring_rubric: Score 3 if: identifies SLE and maps causal connections between all 3 symptoms. Score 2 if: correct diagnosis but missing some connections. Score 1 if: identifies autoimmune but not SLE specifically. Score 0 if: treats symptoms independently.

text: In a supply chain: Factory A supplies components to Factory B and Factory C. Factory B assembles products using components from A and C. Factory C produces sub-assemblies using components from A only. All three sell to Distributor D. Factory A announces a 2-week shutdown for maintenance. Trace all direct and indirect effects through the network.
ground_truth: Direct effects: A->B (B loses components), A->C (C loses components). Indirect: C can't supply B (B doubly hit). B can't produce final products. D loses supply from both B and C. A's shutdown creates a cascade that affects all nodes, with B hit worst (loses both direct and indirect supply).
scoring_rubric: Score 3 if: traces all direct AND indirect effects, identifies B as worst-hit, maps the full cascade. Score 2 if: gets direct effects but misses some indirect cascades. Score 1 if: only identifies direct effects. Score 0 if: misses the interconnected nature.

text: Three laws are in effect: Law X states 'Any citizen may conduct activity Y.' Law Z states 'Activity Y requires a government permit.' Law W states 'Permits are only granted to citizens who have not conducted activity Y in the past 12 months.' A citizen who has never conducted activity Y wants to do so. Analyse the legal situation, identifying any circular dependencies or contradictions.
ground_truth: There is a circular constraint: Q requires government action that references the very activity it governs. A first-time citizen can get a permit (no prior Y), so no contradiction for newcomers. But after conducting Y once, they cannot get a new permit for 12 months. The system is internally consistent but creates a de facto annual licensing regime not explicitly stated.
scoring_rubric: Score 3 if: identifies the circular structure, resolves it for first-timers, and notes the implicit licensing regime. Score 2 if: identifies circularity but incomplete resolution. Score 1 if: feels something is wrong but can't articulate the structure. Score 0 if: treats laws independently.

text: A startup has three co-founders: Alice (CEO, controls fundraising), Bob (CTO, controls product roadmap), and Carol (COO, controls hiring). Alice wants to raise a Series A which requires a demo-ready product. Bob says the product needs 3 more engineers to be demo-ready. Carol says she cannot hire without confirmed funding to guarantee salaries. Map the dependencies and identify the deadlock. Then propose a resolution.
ground_truth: Circular dependency: Fundraising needs product -> Product needs engineers -> Hiring needs funding -> Funding needs product. Resolution options: (1) Bridge loan or convertible note to break the cycle, (2) Alice raises on vision/traction, not demo, (3) Bob scopes a minimal demo achievable with current team, (4) Carol offers equity-only contracts to initial hires.
scoring_rubric: Score 3 if: maps the full circular dependency AND proposes at least 2 viable resolution strategies. Score 2 if: identifies the cycle but weak resolution. Score 1 if: sees individual blockers but misses the cycle. Score 0 if: treats each issue independently.

text: An ecosystem consists of: wolves (apex predator), deer (herbivore, wolf prey), rabbits (herbivore), foxes (predator of rabbits), grass (primary producer), and oak trees (primary producer). Wolves are removed from the ecosystem by hunting. Trace all direct and indirect ecological effects over a 5-year period, identifying any trophic cascades, competitive dynamics, and potential regime shifts.
ground_truth: Wolf removal -> deer population explosion -> overgrazing of grass -> grass decline -> rabbit population declines (less food) -> fox population declines (less prey). Oak seedlings also overgrazed by deer -> forest regeneration fails -> regime shift from mixed ecosystem to degraded grassland. This mirrors the actual Yellowstone trophic cascade.
scoring_rubric: Score 3 if: traces full trophic cascade through multiple levels with temporal dynamics and regime shift. Score 2 if: gets direct effects (deer explosion) but misses downstream cascades. Score 1 if: only identifies wolf-deer relationship. Score 0 if: treats species independently.

text: A university has four departments: Computer Science (CS), Mathematics (Math), Physics, and Engineering. CS shares 30% of courses with Math. Physics requires Math prerequisites for 60% of courses. Engineering requires both Physics and CS courses. Budget cuts force a 25% reduction in Math faculty. Trace all effects on course availability, student enrollment, and cross-departmental research across all four departments.
ground_truth: Math faculty cuts -> fewer Math courses offered -> CS loses 30% shared courses (fewer electives) -> Physics loses prerequisites (60% of courses bottlenecked) -> Engineering doubly hit (both Physics and CS degraded). Student effects: Math enrollment drops, Physics students delayed by prereq bottleneck, Engineering enrollment may drop if degree timeline extends. Research: cross-departmental grants involving Math decline, Physics-Math collaborations disrupted.
scoring_rubric: Score 3 if: traces full cascade through all 4 departments covering courses, enrollment, AND research. Score 2 if: gets department-level effects but misses some cascades. Score 1 if: only identifies direct Math impact. Score 0 if: treats departments independently.
